Advanced Data Management — Project Report

City Mobility Platform · Multi-Database Implementation and Performance Analysis

Student: Abrorkhon Pulotkhonov

Course: Advanced Data Management

Instructor: Assistant Prof. Andrea Campagner

Date: May 2026

GitHub: github.com/YOURUSERNAME/REPONAME

PostgreSQL MongoDB Neo4j Apache Spark · PySpark · GraphFrames

Project Overview

This project implements the data management component of a city-scale electric vehicle rental platform. The same dataset contains Users, Stations, Trips, and Events is modeled and queried three database models (relational, document, graph) and one distributed processing framework. The goal is to compare their efficiency, scalability, and design trade-offs across realistic data volumes.

DimensionValues Tested
Users1,000  ·  10,000  ·  50,000
Trips10,000  ·  50,000  ·  100,000
Events per trip0  ·  2  ·  5  ·  10
Stations50 (fixed)
Total combinations36

The data was randomly synthetically generated in python(Faker) and stored Pandas dataframe which is used to populated the databases.

All 36 scale combinations were benchmarked. Loading time and query execution time are measured independently using time.time(). Memory usage is captured with psutil after each measurement.

Part 1 — Relational vs Document Models

1.1 Data Modeling

Relational Schema — PostgreSQL

users     ( user_id PK, name, surname, birthdate, country )
stations ( station_id PK, name, city, max_capacity )
trips     ( trip_id PK, user_id FK→users,
           start_station_id FK→stations, end_station_id FK→stations,
           start_time, end_time, total_cost )
events    ( event_id PK, trip_id FK→trips, timestamp, type, value )

Every entity lives in its own table, linked by foreign keys. Relationships are expressed via foreign key references. Tables are dropped and recreated in reverse dependency order (events → trips → stations/users) because foreign key constraints prevent deletion of a table that another table still references. Every query that needs information from multiple entities pays a JOIN cost at query time.

Document Schema — MongoDB

users: { user_id, name, surname, birthdate, country }

trips: {
  trip_id, user_id,
  start_station: { station_id, name, city, max_capacity },
  end_station:   { station_id, name, city, max_capacity },
  start_time, end_time, total_cost,
  events: [ { event_id, timestamp, type, value }, ... ]
}

Embedding vs Referencing — Design Decisions

The central question in MongoDB design is whether to embed related data inside a parent document or reference it by ID in a separate collection. The decision must be based on the structure of the database. The relational database(Postgres) has foreign key which can easily join tables and perfectly perform cross table searches, while document model database(mongo) is designed to embed entities poorly performing in cross-collection operations.

Events — embedded inside trips
Events belong to exactly one trip and are never accessed independently. The relationship is one-to-few (max 10 per trip), which is the standard guideline for embedding.

  • Advantage: Q4 and Q3 both run as single-collection scans with no cross-collection access.
  • Trade-off: At max scale (100k trips × 10 events), trip documents hold 1M embedded event objects- a little heavy though accepted because query gains outweigh the size cost.

Stations — embedded inside trips
Station data is needed in every trip read (Q1, Q3, Q4) and rarely changes.

  • Advantage: Q1 and Q3 avoids to use $lookups
  • Trade-off: A station rename would require updating all trip documents that reference it.

Users — are separate collection

1.2 Query Implementation and Performance Comparison

Query 1 — All trips with user information and start/end station names

PostgreSQL — queries_postgres.py
SELECT t.trip_id, u.name, u.surname, u.country,
       s1.name AS start_station, s2.name AS end_station,
       t.start_time, t.end_time, t.total_cost
FROM trips t
JOIN users    u  ON t.user_id          = u.user_id
JOIN stations s1 ON t.start_station_id = s1.station_id
JOIN stations s2 ON t.end_station_id   = s2.station_id

The stations table is joined twice with different aliases (s1 for start, s2 for end) because each trip references two station roles from the same table.

MongoDB — queries_mongo.py
db.trips.aggregate([
  { $lookup: { from: 'users', localField: 'user_id',
               foreignField: 'user_id', as: 'user_info' } },
  { $unwind: '$user_info' },
  { $project: { trip_id:1, 'user_info.name':1, 'user_info.surname':1,
                'start_station.name':1, 'end_station.name':1 } }
])

Station names need no join because they are already embedded inside each trip document. A $lookup is used only for user data. $unwind is required after $lookup because MongoDB always returns joined documents as an array — even when there is exactly one match. $unwind flattens that array into a single object so field access like user_info.name works directly.

Query 2 — All users with number of trips and average duration

PostgreSQL
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, u.surname,
       COUNT(t.trip_id) AS num_trips,
       AVG(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (t.end_time - t.start_time)) / 60) AS avg_duration_minutes
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN trips t ON u.user_id = t.user_id
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name, u.surname

LEFT JOIN is used instead of a regular JOIN. A regular JOIN would silently drop users who have never taken a trip — because there is no matching row in the trips table for them. LEFT JOIN keeps every user regardless, and fills num_trips = 0, avg_duration = NULL for those with no trips.

MongoDB
db.users.aggregate([
  { $lookup: { from:'trips', localField:'user_id',
               foreignField:'user_id', as:'user_trips' } },
  { $project: {
    num_trips: { $size: '$user_trips' },
    avg_duration_minutes: { $avg: {
      $map: { input:'$user_trips', as:'trip',
              in: { $divide: [
                { $subtract: [{$toDate:'$$trip.end_time'},{$toDate:'$$trip.start_time'}] },
                60000 ] } } } }
  }}
])

$map is used before $avg because $avg expects an array of numbers, but user_trips is an array of trip documents. $map transforms each trip document into its duration value first, then $avg averages those numbers. Without $map, $avg would not know what to average from a document.

Query 3 — All stations with number of trips starting and ending there

PostgreSQL
SELECT s.station_id, s.name, s.city,
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM trips t1 WHERE t1.start_station_id = s.station_id) AS trips_starting,
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM trips t2 WHERE t2.end_station_id   = s.station_id) AS trips_ending
FROM stations s

Correlated subqueries are used — one COUNT per direction per station. A double JOIN approach was initially attempted but produced incorrect results: joining trips twice for start and end stations causes row multiplication. A station with 200 starting trips joined to 200 ending trips produces 200 × 200 = 40,000 intermediate rows before GROUP BY — making the counts wildly inflated. Correlated subqueries solve this by counting each direction completely independently.

MongoDB
# Two separate aggregation pipelines:
starting = db.trips.aggregate([
  { $group: { _id: '$start_station.name', trips_starting: { $sum: 1 } } }
])
ending = db.trips.aggregate([
  { $group: { _id: '$end_station.name', trips_ending: { $sum: 1 } } }
])
# Results merged in Python

The embedded station data enables a simple sequential pass, while PostgreSQL must scan and compare for each 50 stations the full trips table.

Query 4 — All trips containing at least one ERROR event

PostgreSQL
SELECT DISTINCT t.trip_id, u.name, u.surname,
       s1.name AS start_station, s2.name AS end_station, ...
FROM trips t
JOIN users    u  ON t.user_id          = u.user_id
JOIN stations s1 ON t.start_station_id = s1.station_id
JOIN stations s2 ON t.end_station_id   = s2.station_id
JOIN events   e  ON t.trip_id          = e.trip_id
WHERE e.type = 'ERROR'

DISTINCT is required. Without it, a trip with 3 ERROR events appears 3 times in results — once per matching event row. The JOIN between trips and events multiplies rows (one output row per event), and DISTINCT collapses them back to one row per trip.

MongoDB
db.trips.find(
  { 'events.type': 'ERROR' },
  { trip_id:1, user_id:1, 'start_station.name':1, ... }
)

MongoDB can query directly on nested array fields using dot notation. No join is needed — all event data is already inside the trip document. No DISTINCT is needed — find() returns one document per trip, not one per event. This is the clearest example of embedding's advantage in the entire project.

Full Performance Results — PostgreSQL vs MongoDB

Q4 results depend heavily on events per trip — when events = 0, PostgreSQL wins because there are no ERROR events to filter and no events JOIN overhead. When events > 0, MongoDB's embedded array filter consistently wins.

Performance Results — PostgreSQL ↔ MongoDB (seconds)

Left value = PostgreSQL · Right value = MongoDB · Green = faster value

UsersTripsEventsQ1Q2Q3Q4
1,00010,0000 0.174↔2.316 0.013↔0.094 0.070↔0.009 0.002↔0.004
1,00010,00010 0.194↔2.693 0.012↔0.234 0.102↔0.025 0.287↔0.108
1,00050,0000 0.951↔13.870 0.042↔1.014 0.908↔0.112 0.004↔0.100
1,000100,00010 1.816↔27.207 0.096↔2.151 1.619↔0.288 1.927↔0.973
10,00010,0000 0.191↔2.658 0.058↔0.656 0.142↔0.013 0.003↔0.007
10,00050,0002 0.922↔14.171 0.097↔1.383 0.870↔0.122 0.673↔0.300
10,000100,00010 1.782↔26.980 0.251↔2.706 1.558↔0.252 1.965↔1.150
50,00010,0000 0.205↔2.731 0.291↔1.995 0.121↔0.012 0.003↔0.006
50,00050,00010 0.916↔13.881 0.280↔3.371 0.690↔0.086 1.107↔0.416
50,000100,0000 1.811↔27.659 0.432↔4.545 1.373↔0.171 0.004↔0.118

Figure 1 — PostgreSQL vs MongoDB: query execution time averaged over all user/event scale combinations

Figure 2 — Scalability comparison: execution time vs number of trips

Figure 3 — Impact of events per trip on Q4 execution time

Per-Query Summary

QueryWinnerPostgreSQL behaviorMongoDB behavior
Q1PostgreSQL 0.17s → 1.83s. Native 3-way indexed JOIN. Scales linearly with trips. 2.3s → 27.7s. $lookup iterates per document. Grows super-linearly at scale.
Q2PostgreSQL 0.01s → 0.46s. GROUP BY with indexed FK — single-pass optimized operation. 0.09s → 4.82s. $lookup overhead grows with both user and trip count.
Q3MongoDB 0.07s → 1.66s. Two correlated subqueries — O(stations × trips) complexity. 0.009s → 0.19s. Two sequential $group passes — O(trips). No cross-collection join.
Q4Mixed Grows with events/trip (0.002s → 2.08s). JOIN events table grows with event count. Flat once events > 0. Direct array filter unaffected by event count. Slower only when events = 0.

1.3 Schema Evolution — Adding battery_level to BATTERY Events

The requirement is to add a battery_level field (integer, 0-100) to all events of type BATTERY. This scenario tests how each system handles a partial schema change — one that affects only a subset of records.

PostgreSQL — DDL migration required
-- Step 1: add the column (locks the events table during execution)
ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN battery_level INTEGER
    CHECK (battery_level BETWEEN 0 AND 100);

-- Step 2: enforce the field is present only for BATTERY events
ALTER TABLE events ADD CONSTRAINT chk_battery
    CHECK (type != 'BATTERY' OR battery_level IS NOT NULL);

The ALTER TABLE statement adds the new column to every row in the events table — not just BATTERY events. This means:

  • The table is locked during migration, blocking all reads and writes. At 1 million rows (max scale: 100k trips × 10 events), this causes visible downtime in a production system.
  • All existing BATTERY rows are left with NULL in battery_level and must be backfilled with actual values through a separate UPDATE statement.
  • GPS, ERROR, and DELAY events permanently carry an empty battery_level column — wasted storage space across 75% of all event rows.
MongoDB — no migration needed
-- New BATTERY events simply include the field:
{ type: 'BATTERY', timestamp: '...', value: 80.5, battery_level: 75 }

-- Old BATTERY events without battery_level remain valid.
-- Query only events that have the field:
db.trips.find({ 'events.type': 'BATTERY',
                'events.battery_level': { $exists: true } })

MongoDB's schema-on-read model means documents are valid regardless of which fields they contain. Adding battery_level to new BATTERY events requires no migration — the field simply appears in new documents and is absent from old ones. Old BATTERY events remain valid. There is no table lock, no backfill, and no impact on GPS, ERROR, or DELAY events whatsoever.

AspectPostgreSQLMongoDB
Change requiredALTER TABLE — affects entire events tableNo change — add field to new documents only
Scope of changeAll rows affected (GPS, ERROR, DELAY too)Only new BATTERY event documents
Downtime riskYes — table lock during migrationNone — old and new documents coexist
Old BATTERY recordsFilled with NULL — must be backfilledRemain unchanged — field simply absent
Evolvability verdictLower — rigid schema forces full migrationHigher — flexible schema, zero overhead
Winner: MongoDB. For partial schema evolution — adding a field to only one event type — the document model is clearly superior. PostgreSQL must alter the entire table structure regardless of how narrow the change is. MongoDB handles it at the application level with no structural change at all.

1.4 Spark-based Implementation — Query 2

Query 2 is implemented using Spark DataFrames — the higher-level abstraction over RDDs. DataFrames were chosen over RDDs because the query is a standard groupBy + aggregation + join. Spark's Catalyst optimizer automatically finds the best execution plan for this pattern. RDDs would require manually implementing the grouping and joining logic with map/reduce operations, producing more complex code for no performance benefit.

spark_doc.py — core logic
# Step 1: compute trip duration in minutes
trips_with_duration = trips_spark.withColumn('duration_minutes',
    (F.unix_timestamp('end_time',   'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss') -
     F.unix_timestamp('start_time', 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss')) / 60)

# Step 2: group by user — count trips and average duration
trips_grouped = trips_with_duration.groupBy('user_id').agg(
    F.count('trip_id').alias('num_trips'),
    F.avg('duration_minutes').alias('avg_duration_minutes'))

# Step 3: LEFT join with users to include users with zero trips
result = users_spark.join(trips_grouped, on='user_id', how='left')
result = result.fillna({'num_trips': 0, 'avg_duration_minutes': 0.0})

Three key decisions in this implementation:

  • unix_timestamp() for duration: Spark cannot subtract timestamps directly. unix_timestamp() converts each string to Unix epoch seconds, allowing simple subtraction. Divided by 60 for minutes.
  • LEFT join from users: Same reasoning as PostgreSQL — users with zero trips must appear in results. Without it they are silently dropped.
  • fillna() after join: Users with no trips produce NULL for num_trips and avg_duration. fillna() replaces these with 0 for clean, consistent output.

An important benchmarking detail: DataFrames are cached and forced with.count() before timing starts. Spark is lazy — it does nothing until an action is triggered. Without this, the timer would include data loading time, making the comparison unfair.

Spark vs MongoDB vs PostgreSQL — Q2 Scalability

UsersTripsEventsPostgreSQL (s)MongoDB (s)Spark (s)
1,00010,00000.0130.0941.700
1,00010,00020.0120.2290.413
1,00050,00050.0470.9040.304
10,00050,00020.0971.3830.429
10,000100,000100.2512.7060.435
50,00050,000100.2803.3710.562
50,000100,00010 0.4584.8240.632

Figure 4 — Query 2: Spark vs MongoDB vs PostgreSQL. Left: at 10k trips the crossover is visible — MongoDB starts faster at 1k users (0.19s vs 0.72s) then Spark takes over at 10k users. Right: at 100k trips Spark is always faster.

The crossover pattern reveals something important about both systems. At very small scale (1k users / 10k trips), MongoDB's $lookup is fast enough that Spark's fixed setup overhead dominates. As scale grows — more users means more $lookup iterations — MongoDB degrades linearly while Spark stays roughly flat because its execution plan reads all trips once, groups in memory, then does a single join.

PostgreSQL remains fastest throughout. Its GROUP BY with indexed foreign keys is a decades-optimized single-node operation that is very hard to beat within its range. Spark would overtake PostgreSQL at much larger scales (millions of rows) where single-node processing becomes the bottleneck and horizontal distribution across a real cluster starts to pay off. On local machine, Spark simulates parallelism using multiple CPU cores but cannot achieve true distributed speedup.

Part 2 — Graph Model

2.1 Graph Schema Design — Neo4j

Node types:
  (:USER)    — user_id, name, surname, birthdate, country
  (:STATION) — station_id, name, city, max_capacity
  (:TRIP)    — trip_id, start_time, end_time, total_cost

Edge types:
  (:USER)–[:PERFORMED]→(:TRIP)
  (:TRIP)–[:STARTS_AT]→(:STATION)
  (:TRIP)–[:ENDS_AT]→(:STATION)

Two design decisions require explanation:

  • TRIP as a node rather than an edge: The intuitive model might make trips edges between users and stations. However, a trip connects to two stations (start and end) via different relationship types. Modeling a trip as an edge would force choosing one station endpoint. Making TRIP a node allows clean expression of both STARTS_AT and ENDS_AT, and also lets us store trip attributes (cost, time) on the node itself.
  • Events are not included in the graph: Neither graph query requires event data. Including events would add up to 1 million additional nodes at maximum scale (100k trips × 10 events), inflating graph size with no benefit to any traversal. The graph is kept minimal — only entities and relationships required by the queries.

Loading is performed with UNWIND batch transactions of 2,000 records per transaction. Indexes on user_id, trip_id, and station_id are created before loading relationships. Without indexes, each MATCH statement in relationship creation performs a full graph scan. With indexes, each lookup is O(log n). Nodes must be loaded before relationships because MATCH can only find nodes that already exist.

Figure 5 — Neo4j graph visualization. Left: full graph with all nodes. Right: zoomed view showing USER (purple), TRIP (pink), and STATION (teal) nodes with PERFORMED, STARTS_AT, and ENDS_AT relationships.

2.2 Neo4j Queries

Query 1 — All stations reachable through a given user's trips

Cypher — queries_neo4j.py
MATCH (u:USER {user_id: $user_id})-[:PERFORMED]->
      (t:TRIP)-[:STARTS_AT|ENDS_AT]->(s:STATION)
RETURN DISTINCT s.name AS station_name, s.city AS city

This is a 2-hop graph traversal: USER → TRIP → STATION. Key decisions:

  • The pipe operator [:STARTS_AT|ENDS_AT] matches either relationship type in a single pattern. A trip connects to two stations — one via STARTS_AT and one via ENDS_AT. Using | covers both in a single MATCH instead of requiring two separate patterns UNIONed together.
  • DISTINCT prevents duplicates when a user made multiple trips between the same pair of stations — without it, those stations appear once per trip.
  • It is fast: Neo4j stores relationships as direct pointers between nodes. Finding all PERFORMED edges from a user node follows the node's adjacency list directly — it does not scan an index or compare rows. This is fundamentally different from a relational JOIN.

Query 2 — Top 3 most important stations by total trip connections

Cypher — queries_neo4j.py
MATCH (s:STATION)
WITH s,
     COUNT {(s)<-[:STARTS_AT]-()} AS trips_starting,
     COUNT {(s)<-[:ENDS_AT]-()} AS trips_ending
RETURN s.name, trips_starting, trips_ending,
       trips_starting + trips_ending AS total_trips
ORDER BY total_trips DESC
LIMIT 3

COUNT {} subqueries count incoming relationships per node directly. The alternative — using OPTIONAL MATCH for both directions then GROUP BY — causes the same row multiplication problem as the double JOIN in PostgreSQL Q3: a station with 200 starting and 200 ending trips would produce 40,000 intermediate rows before grouping. COUNT {} avoids this by counting each direction independently within its own subquery scope.

The traversal direction matters: (s)<-[:STARTS_AT]-() means arrows pointing into s, which is how trips connect to stations in the schema. Reversing the arrow would search for edges going the wrong direction and return nothing.

Trips (edges)Load timeQ1 avg timeQ2 avg time
10,000~4.1s0.124s0.032s
50,000~14.6s0.066s0.027s
100,000~28.8s0.023s0.066s

Neo4j shows a striking pattern: query execution is consistently under 100ms at all scales, but loading time grows significantly with trips. This is the graph database trade-off — expensive write-time index construction pays off with millisecond read-time traversal. Loading takes 28.8s at 100k trips because each trip requires creating one TRIP node and three relationship edges, each matched via indexed lookups.

2.3 Spark GraphFrames — PageRank and Connected Components

GraphFrames requires the graph as two DataFrames: a vertices DataFrame with a mandatory id column (String type), and an edges DataFrame with mandatory src and dst columns. The stations-only subgraph is built by mapping each trip to a directed edge between its start and end station. Trip properties, users, and events are all discarded — only station connectivity matters for these algorithms.

spark_graph.py — building the graph
vertices = stations_spark.select(
    F.col('station_id').cast(StringType()).alias('id'),
    F.col('name'), F.col('city'))

edges = trips_spark.select(
    F.col('start_station_id').cast(StringType()).alias('src'),
    F.col('end_station_id').cast(StringType()).alias('dst'))

graph = GraphFrame(vertices, edges)

Station IDs are cast to String because GraphFrames requires vertex IDs to be strings — this is a library constraint, not a design choice.

PageRank

spark_graph.py
pagerank = graph.pageRank(resetProbability=0.15, tol=0.01)
top3 = pagerank.vertices.orderBy(F.col('pagerank').desc()).limit(3)

PageRank simulates a random traveler moving through the station graph. With 85% probability they follow an edge (take a trip to a connected station). With 15% probability they jump to a completely random station. resetProbability=0.15 is the standard value used in all PageRank implementations including the original Google algorithm.

tol=0.01 controls convergence — the algorithm stops when scores change by less than 0.01 between iterations. Unlike Neo4j Q2 which ranks stations by raw trip count, PageRank considers connection quality: a station linked to other heavily-used stations scores higher than one with the same trip count but connected only to isolated stations.

Connected Components

spark_graph.py
spark.sparkContext.setCheckpointDir('C:/tmp/spark_checkpoints')
components = graph.connectedComponents(algorithm='graphx')

Connected Components identifies groups of stations where every station can reach every other through some chain of trips.

Why use Spark GraphFrames instead of Neo4j's built-in graph algorithms? Neo4j can also run PageRank and Connected Components. The difference is scale: Neo4j runs on a single machine and cannot scale beyond available RAM. GraphFrames distributes computation across a cluster, making it suitable for billion-node graphs. On this project's local[*] setup the difference is overhead rather than speedup, but the architecture is designed for real distributed environments.

Figure 6 — Spark GraphFrames: PageRank vs Connected Components execution time across scales

Both algorithms show roughly flat times across trip scales. This is expected: the graph has only 50 station vertices regardless of trip count. Graph algorithm complexity is determined by vertex count, not edge count in this case. PageRank (~4-5s) is consistently slower than Connected Components (~0.8s) because PageRank requires multiple convergence iterations while Connected Components resolves in fewer passes.

Part 3 — Partitioning and Replication

3.1 Partitioning

Two strategies are considered for partitioning the trips collection: by user_id or by start_station_id.

Partitioning by User

All trips belonging to a given user are stored on the same partition.

QueryImpact
Q1 — trips with user infoBenefit — if filtered by user, all their trips are local to one partition
Q2 — users with trip statsFull benefit — all of a user's trips are on the same shard
Q3 — station trip countsNegative — trips for a given station are spread across all user shards; full scatter required
Q4 — ERROR eventsNegative — ERROR trips can belong to any user; all shards must be scanned
Neo4j Q1 — reachable stationsFull benefit — user's PERFORMED edges and connected TRIPs are co-located
Neo4j Q2 — top stationsNegative — trips per station distributed across all user shards
PageRankNegative — edges cross all shards regardless of user
Connected ComponentsNegative — same as PageRank

Partitioning by Start Station

All trips starting at a given station are stored on the same partition.

QueryImpact
Q1 — trips with user infoNegative — a user's trips spread across all station shards; full scatter required
Q2 — users with trip statsNegative — same as Q1, user trips distributed by start station
Q3 — station trip countsPartial — trips_starting is local; trips_ending requires cross-shard access
Q4 — ERROR eventsNegative — ERROR trips can start at any station; full scatter required
Neo4j Q1 — reachable stationsNegative — user's trips distributed by start station; traversal crosses shards
Neo4j Q2 — top stationsPartial — STARTS_AT counts local; ENDS_AT requires scatter
PageRankPartial — outgoing edges local; incoming require scatter
Connected ComponentsPartial — same as PageRank
Recommendation: Partition by user_id. The most frequent and latency-sensitive queries are user-centric Q1 (users viewing their trip history) and Q2 (user statistics). These are interactive queries where users expect fast responses. Q3 is a periodic analytics query where scatter-gather overhead is acceptable. Neo4j Q1 also benefits directly from user-based partitioning. No strategy is perfect for all queries, but user_id partitioning optimizes for the majority of real-world use cases.

3.2 Replication

Setup: single-leader asynchronous replication with 1 primary and 2 secondary replicas. Writes are acknowledged by the primary before propagating to secondaries. Secondaries may lag by milliseconds to seconds depending on write load and network conditions.

QueryRisk if served by secondaryInconsistency typeSeverity
Q1 — Trip history A recently completed trip may not have replicated yet. A user checking their history immediately after a trip may not see it. Read-your-writes violationMedium
Q2 — User stats Trip count and avg duration may be understated if recent trips have not replicated. Two reads from different secondaries could return different counts. Monotonic read violation possibleMedium
Q3 — Station counts Station trip counts may be slightly stale. For a reporting dashboard, counts off by a few trips are generally acceptable. Stale readLow
Q4 — ERROR events A trip where an ERROR event just occurred may not appear on the secondary. In a vehicle platform, this could mean a vehicle malfunction goes undetected until the lag resolves. Critical stale readHigh ⚠
Neo4j Q1 — Reachable stations New trip edges (PERFORMED, STARTS_AT, ENDS_AT) may not yet be replicated. Graph traversal returns incomplete stations — missing those from recent trips. Incomplete graph traversalMedium
Neo4j Q2 — Top stations Station importance counts may be stale. The top 3 stations are unlikely to change dramatically between replication cycles. Stale aggregateLow
PageRank Computed on a stale graph snapshot. Slightly altered rankings are acceptable for periodic analytics — not real-time. Stale graph snapshotLow
Connected Components Component structure changes only when stations are added or isolated. Stale data has minimal practical impact for periodic analysis. Stale graph snapshotLow
Routing recommendations: Always route Q4 to the primary — ERROR detection is safety-critical. Route Q1 to primary with primaryPreferred — users expect to see their own recent trips immediately. Route Q2, Q3, Neo4j Q2, PageRank, Connected Components to secondaries to distribute read load while accepting minor lag for analytics.

Loading Time and Memory Analysis

Database10k trips, 0 events100k trips, 10 eventsGrowth factorNotes
PostgreSQL2.6s187.1s72×Events table reaches 1M rows — each event is a separate INSERT row
MongoDB0.5s61.4s123×Events embedded in trips — larger documents, but one insert_many call
Neo4j4.1s32.0s7.8×Events not stored in graph — load grows only with nodes and edges

PostgreSQL is heavily impacted by events per trip because each event is a separate table row — at 100k trips × 10 events = 1 million individual INSERT statements via executemany. The 72× growth factor makes this the most event-sensitive database for loading.

MongoDB growth is driven by document size increase. Embedding events means each trip document grows from ~200 bytes (no events) to ~1.4KB (10 events). Despite the growth factor appearing large, the absolute time is still faster than PostgreSQL because insert_many writes all events as part of the trip document in a single batch operation.

Neo4j shows the lowest growth factor (7.8×) because events are not stored at all — loading time depends only on nodes and relationship edges, which do not change with events per trip.

Figure 7 — Loading time comparison across databases (averaged over all user/event combinations)

Figure 8 — Loading time vs events per trip at 100k trips — shows how each database is affected by growing event data

Figure 9 — Memory usage by database and trip count

Engineering Challenges

1. Python 3.14 incompatibility with PySpark

Python 3.14 / PySpark incompatibility: Python 3.14 is the latest version but PySpark 3.5.5 is not yet compatible with it — the distutils module that PySpark relies on was removed in 3.14. Downgrading to Python 3.11 resolved the Spark issue but introduced a chain of dependency conflicts with OpenJDK and other libraries that had to be resolved one by one before the environment was fully functional

2. MongoDB $lookup without indexes — queries exceeding 1,000 seconds

The first benchmark run executed $lookup without indexes on user_id. At 1k users / 10k trips, Q1 took approximately 22 seconds. At 10k users / 50k trips, the script ran for over 1,000 seconds before being terminated. Root cause: without an index, $lookup performs a full users collection scan for every trip document — O(trips × users). At 50k trips × 10k users = 500 million comparisons.

Adding explicit indexes on user_id in both collections reduced the same combination from ~1,018 seconds to 13.7 seconds — a 74x speedup. This highlights a key difference from PostgreSQL: PRIMARY KEY automatically creates a B-tree index, while MongoDB only auto-indexes the internal _id field. Any other field used for lookups must be indexed explicitly.

Figure 10 — MongoDB Q1 execution time with vs without user_id index. On the right foto 100k is the combination when I started using index.

UsersTripsWithout Index (s)With Index (s)Speedup
1,00010,00022.02.49.2×
1,00050,000112.113.88.1×
1,000100,000223.725.78.7×
10,00010,000205.82.775.4×
10,00050,000~1,01813.7~74×
50,00010,0002.72.7~1× (users >> trips, few matches per scan)
50,000100,00026.927.5~1× (same reason)

An interesting pattern: at 50k users, the index provides virtually no speedup. When users are many relative to trips, each user has very few trips. The $lookup scan per trip is short regardless of indexing — there are simply not many users to scan per match. The index provides the most benefit when users are few (1k-10k) but trips are many, making the scan users per trip prohibitively large.

3. Neo4j row-by-row insertion — too slow at scale

The initial Neo4j loader used individual session.run() calls per record. For 100k trips with 3 relationship types (PERFORMED, STARTS_AT, ENDS_AT), this meant over 400,000 individual database round-trips — each incurring network overhead and transaction cost. The fix was batch UNWIND transactions: a single Cypher query receives 2,000 records as a parameter and inserts all in one transaction, reducing round-trips by ~200x and bringing loading time from hours to minutes. Indexes were also pre-created before loading — without them, each MATCH in relationship creation performs a full graph scan.

4. Row multiplication in Q3 with double JOIN

The first PostgreSQL implementation of Q3 joined the trips table twice with LEFT JOIN — once for starting trips, once for ending trips — then GROUP BY station. A station with 200 starting and 200 ending trips produced 200 × 200 = 40,000 intermediate rows before GROUP BY, making counts wildly inflated. The fix was correlated subqueries: one independent COUNT(*) per direction per station. Each subquery runs exactly once per station, counting in one direction only, with no interference from the other.

Conclusion

Figure 11 — All queries at maximum scale (50k users, 100k trips, 10 events/trip)

Use CaseBest ChoiceReason
Cross-entity joins (Q1, Q2)PostgreSQLNative indexed JOIN — 1.83s and 0.46s at max scale
Aggregation on embedded data (Q3)MongoDBSequential scan — 0.19s at max scale vs 1.66s for PostgreSQL
Event filtering on embedded arrays (Q4)MongoDBSingle find() with array filter — no JOIN, no DISTINCT
Schema evolution — partial field addMongoDBSchema-on-read — no migration, no table lock, no backfill
Graph traversal — connectivity queriesNeo4jPointer-based storage — under 10ms at all scales
Distributed graph analyticsSpark GraphFramesFlat ~4s PageRank regardless of scale — scales to real clusters
Scalable aggregation at cluster scaleSparkConstant execution time locally; outperforms single-node DBs at millions of rows

The results confirm that no single database paradigm is optimal for all queries. The decision to embed stations and events inside trip documents was decisive — it made Q4 trivial in MongoDB and gave MongoDB an 8x advantage on Q3. However, the decision to reference users by user_id (which was the correct design choice to avoid update anomalies) created the main performance bottleneck in Q1 and Q2.

The Neo4j results demonstrate the classic graph database trade-off: millisecond query execution at the cost of expensive write-time loading. The Spark GraphFrames flat curves across all trip scales illustrate distributed computing principles — when the bottleneck is the fixed vertex count (50 stations), execution time is constant regardless of edge count.

A production city mobility platform would likely use a polyglot architecture: PostgreSQL for user management and trip history, MongoDB for real-time event processing and schema flexibility, Neo4j for mobility pattern analysis, and Spark for large-scale periodic analytics.
